metadata.summary: Five-stage audit for WCAG 2.1/2.2 AA: scan → triage → keyboard → screen reader → report. Automated tools catch 30-40%; human testing stages 3-4 mandatory.

content.title: Accessibility Evaluation
content.summary: A five-stage audit process for WCAG 2.1/2.2 AA conformance: automated scan (axe, Pa11y, Lighthouse) → triage → scripted keyboard testing (Playwright) → screen reader verification (NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver) → structured report. Automated tools catch 30-40% of issues; stages 3-4 are mandatory to reach meaningful coverage. Screen reader verification requires a human — no LLM can reproduce AT speech buffers.

why.p: "axe-clean = compliant" is the most common audit failure. Automated tools are blind to ARIA misuse, focus-order problems in modals, live-region behavior, and cognitive accessibility. Legal risk (ADA Title II, EAA, procurement VPAT requirements) requires structured evidence: per-criterion findings, remediation recommendations citing W3C techniques, and POUR-grouped tables — not just tool output.

MegaLinter (oxsecurity, fork of GitHub Super-Linter) is the only one-call surface that covers 69 languages, 23 formats and 21 tooling formats in one shot. The parallel Python multiprocessing engine is meaningfully faster than Super-Linter's sequential bash and produces SARIF that drops directly into GitHub Code Scanning's UI.

list.item: Pre-release WCAG 2.1/2.2 AA audit before launch.
Regression check after design system upgrades or major refactors.
Procurement / RFP responses requiring VPAT or ACR documentation.
Post-incident remediation when a complaint or lawsuit names specific WCAG criteria.
CI gating on PRs touching shared library components.
Early lo-fi wireframe stage — use accessibility-first-design heuristics, not a full audit.
Pure ATAG or UAAG compliance — different rule sets; this methodology covers WCAG content/UI scope only.
Native iOS/Android — use Accessibility Inspector (Xcode) and Accessibility Scanner (Android).
PDF or document accessibility — use PAC 2024 or Adobe Acrobat Pro checker.

section.name: accessibility-evaluation-five-stage-process
section.p: Structured five-stage audit process for WCAG 2.1/2.2 AA: automated scan, triage, keyboard testing, screen reader verification, and structured report — with tool pairings and issue priority tiers.

rule.statement: Run automated scanning (axe-core, Pa11y, Lighthouse) as stage 1, but never claim compliance based on automated results alone — tools catch 30-40% of issues.
rule.rationale: Deque and WebAIM data (2024) confirm automated tools miss the majority of issues: ARIA misuse, focus order in modals, live-region behavior, cognitive accessibility, and text-over-image contrast are invisible to headless scanners.

rule.statement: Pin the axe-core version per repo; rule additions in minor versions create phantom regressions that derail review cycles.
rule.rationale: axe-core releases new rules in minor versions. An "axe-clean" baseline at v4.8 may produce violations at v4.9 for the same unmodified code, causing false regression alerts.

rule.statement: Test all of: Tab navigation reaching every interactive element, visible focus at all times, logical tab order matching visual flow, skip links, no keyboard traps, and documented shortcuts not conflicting with AT shortcuts.
rule.rationale: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.1.1, 2.4.3, 2.4.7, and 2.4.11 cover these criteria. Each requires separate verification; automated tools cover none of them reliably.

rule.statement: For SPA frameworks, audit after route transitions and after async state changes; focus management at route change is the #1 SPA accessibility failure.
rule.rationale: Single-page apps frequently drop focus to the document body after navigation, forcing screen reader users to start re-reading from the top of the page.

rule.statement: Use established AT pairings for screen reader testing: NVDA + Firefox, JAWS + Chrome, VoiceOver + Safari on Mac, VoiceOver + Safari on iOS, TalkBack + Chrome on Android.
rule.rationale: AT behavior differs by browser pairing. A finding present in NVDA+Firefox may not reproduce in JAWS+Chrome — and vice versa. Pairings are the industry-standard evidence baseline for VPAT/ACR documentation.

rule.statement: Never accept a screen reader finding from an agent that does not include an audio recording or verbatim speech transcript as evidence; fabricated AT transcripts are undetectable and create false compliance records.
rule.rationale: LLMs cannot reproduce NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver speech buffers. Agent-invented transcripts appear plausible but are not evidence of conformance.

rule.statement: Classify every finding into Critical (blocks access completely, fix immediately), High (major barrier with difficult workaround, this sprint), Medium (creates difficulty, next sprint), or Low (enhancement, backlog).
rule.rationale: Consistent priority classification lets stakeholders make resource decisions and produces comparable metrics across audit cycles. Unclassified findings stall remediation planning.

example.title: Issue Priority Example
example.note: Missing form label (SC 1.3.1) → Critical: screen reader users cannot identify the field; no workaround. Missing skip link (SC 2.4.1) → High: keyboard users must tab through full navigation on every page.

section.name: agent-pipeline-roles-and-gotchas
section.p: Stage 1 (scan) and Stage 5 (report) are fully agent-owned. Stage 3 (keyboard) is scripted via Playwright. Stage 4 (screen reader) is human-in-the-loop — no LLM can replicate NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver speech buffers. The orchestrator fans out to: a scanner subagent (axe + Pa11y JSON output), a triage subagent (dedup + WCAG mapping), and a writer subagent (markdown report).

rule.statement: Treat each WCAG criterion as a separate test case with a stable ID so re-runs produce diffs, not full rewrites.
rule.rationale: Without stable IDs, every re-run generates a new report that cannot be compared to the previous one. Diffs are the only way to track remediation progress and detect regressions.

example.title: Triage Prompt Pattern
example.note: Triage: "Given axe-core JSON (violations[]), produce ## Detailed Findings. For each violation: WCAG SC, priority (Critical/High/Medium/Low), location (selector + page URL), recommendation (cite W3C technique G/H/F number), and minimal HTML before/after. Group by Perceivable/Operable/Understandable/Robust."

example.title: Keyboard Trace Prompt Pattern
example.note: Keyboard trace: "Given Playwright keyboard-only trace, emit a row in the keyboard-testing table for: Tab navigation, Focus visible, Logical order, Skip links, Traps, Shortcuts. Pass = boolean + evidence (frame index)."

antipattern.description: Agent generating alt="Description" placeholders that pass automated checks but fail SC 1.1.1 for decorative or context-specific images.
antipattern.reason: Placeholder alt text passes the "attribute present" check but fails the "alternative text conveys equivalent information" criterion. Require the agent to cite the source content the alt is derived from.

antipattern.description: Agent fixing contrast by editing CSS variables without checking dark-mode token consistency.
antipattern.reason: Token-layer edits propagate to all modes. A contrast fix in light mode can create a new violation in dark mode. Require diffs with both modes regenerated.

antipattern.description: Agent mass-adding aria-label to elements that already have visible text labels.
antipattern.reason: Adding aria-label to an element with visible text creates a SC 2.5.3 (Label in Name) violation if the label text differs. Lint for accessible-name vs visible-text mismatch before applying.

antipattern.description: Agent closing findings as "won't fix" without a human accessibility lead sign-off with documented rationale.
antipattern.reason: Suppressed findings without rationale create phantom compliance. Legal and procurement reviewers will ask for documented risk acceptance; agents cannot provide it.

antipattern.description: Scanning logged-out fallback pages instead of authenticated application states.
antipattern.reason: Authenticated pages have additional dynamic content, modals, and forms not visible to unauthenticated scans. Clean reports from unauthenticated pages are false negatives.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools for accessibility scanning and testing:

list.item: axe-core CLI: Headless WCAG scan, JSON output. Install: npm i -g @axe-core/cli (https://github.com/dequelabs/axe-core-npm)
Pa11y / Pa11y-CI: Batch URL audits, sitemap-driven. Install: npm i -g pa11y pa11y-ci (https://github.com/pa11y/pa11y)
Lighthouse CI: Accessibility category in CI. Install: npm i -g @lhci/cli (https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse-ci)
WAVE API: Page-level scan with screenshots (paid). https://wave.webaim.org/api/
IBM Equal Access Checker: Cross-platform CLI + browser (https://github.com/IBMa/equal-access)
Playwright + @axe-core/playwright: Scripted scans tied to user flows. Install: npm i -D @axe-core/playwright
color-contrast-checker: Token / palette validation in CI. Install: npm i color-contrast-checker
html-validate: Catches role/aria errors statically (https://html-validate.org)
accessibility-checker (npm): Wrapper for IBM ruleset. Install: npm i accessibility-checker
Deque axe DevTools Pro: Best false-positive rate; agent-friendly REST API + axe-core OSS. "Intelligent Guided Tests" require human.
Siteimprove: Strong for large sites; partial agent-friendly REST API; expensive.
Tenon.io: Lightweight scans, JSON only, agent-friendly REST API.
Stark: SaaS / Figma plug-in for design-time contrast and vision sim; limited agent support.
TPGi ARC Toolkit: Manual + automated, agent-friendly Selenium hooks, VPAT-friendly.
BrowserStack Accessibility Testing: Cross-browser real-device scans, CI integration.
AssistivLabs: Remote NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver — still human-driven, partial agent support.
AccessiBe / UserWay: SaaS overlay — anti-pattern. Avoid; widely litigated, does not produce conformance.
Evinced: Component-level dynamic analysis, agent-friendly CLI + CI.
Microsoft Accessibility Insights: Free OSS desktop + Web, AI-assistant-readable HTML reports, FastPass automatable.
Run scans on the rendered DOM of representative states (modal open, error, empty, loaded), not just the initial route — single-snapshot scans miss the majority of dynamic-content issues.
Pin axe-core version per repo; rule additions between minor versions cause "phantom regressions" that derail review cycles.
Map every finding to a WCAG SC and a remediation technique (G/H/F numbers from W3C); reviewers reject reports that just cite axe rule ids.
Maintain a shared "known-issues" allowlist with expiry dates, not infinite suppressions; review quarterly.
Combine NVDA + Firefox, JAWS + Chrome, VoiceOver + Safari in AT testing — pairings change announcement behaviour materially.
Re-test the fix in the same flow that exposed it, not in isolation; refactors often re-break neighbouring components.
For SPA frameworks, audit after route transitions and after async state changes — focus management is the #1 SPA failure.
VPAT/ACR drafts: preserve evidence (screenshots, DOM snapshots, AT transcripts) so claims can be defended in procurement.

template.purpose: Accessibility audit report: executive summary, methodology, POUR findings tables, per-issue template
Playwright + axe-core scanner: emits per-page JSON consumed by triage agent
